metadata.title: Agentic Research Course
metadata.author: Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.
metadata.description: Week 10: Build Your Own -- skills, agents, and the researcher's plugin. The six building blocks (skill, agent, command, hook, MCP, plugin), two questions that pick the right one for any job, building a skill with /skill-development, testing that it triggers, the skill-reviewer agent, and forking research-skills to make it your own. The capstone: tool user in, tool builder out.

elements.content: # Agentic Research Course
Week 10: Build Your Own -- Skills, Agents, and the Researcher's Plugin

**Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.**
Assistant Project Scientist, Swartz Center for Computational Neuroscience
UC San Diego

[Open Science Collective](https://osc.earth)

Course: [courses.osc.earth/agentic-research](https://courses.osc.earth/agentic-research/) | Discord: [discord.gg/5dWJCUmUww](https://discord.gg/5dWJCUmUww) | Recording: published to YouTube within 48 h

slides.speakerNotes:
- The capstone. Welcome to the last session.

- For nine weeks you used tools other people built. Today you learn the six kinds of tool a coding agent can run, which one to reach for, and how to build the simplest of them -- a skill.

- The arc: name the six building blocks, then build one live with /skill-development.

- The promise to land at the end: you arrived a tool user; you leave a tool builder.

- Format: ~30 min, one small live demo, 15 min Q&A.

elements.content: ## Where We Are -- and the Turn

items.text: **Weeks 1-4 -- git, Claude Code, project management, CI/CD.** The safety net and the agent.
**Weeks 5-9 -- lit review, grants, manuscripts, figures, BIDS.** A whole research pipeline, one workflow at a time.
**Every week you ran a tool -- and never named what kind it was.**
**Today: name all six building blocks, learn which to reach for, and build one.** You arrived a tool user. You leave a tool builder.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 3x to reveal fragments]

- Recap the arc fast: 1-4 the engine and safety net; 5-9 the writing-and-data pipeline.

- The turn: everything so far assumed the tool already existed. This week you build the tool.

- Land the closing line slowly -- it is the thesis of the whole session and the course.

elements.content: ## Skill? Agent? Command? Hook? MCP? Plugin?

elements.alt: Six overlapping, tilted pills -- Skill (green), Plugin (blue), Agent (purple), MCP (red), Command (teal), Hook (amber) -- scattered with faint question marks on the left, a blue arrow pointing right to a card titled TWO QUESTIONS: 1. Who triggers it? 2. Where does it run?

elements.content: These six words are not interchangeable. **You do not need six definitions -- you need two questions.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- This is the real question every researcher has after a few weeks with Claude Code.

- Resist defining all six up front; promise the two-question shortcut instead.

- The colors here are a legend used the whole deck: Skill green, Agent purple, Command teal, Hook amber, MCP red, Plugin blue.

elements.content: ## Two Questions That Sort Them All

elements.alt: A 2-by-3 grid. Rows are who triggers it: Claude, You, An event, Always-on. Columns are where it runs: Main chat, Its own context, Outside the model. Skill sits at Claude x Main chat; Agent at Claude x Own context; Command at You x Main chat; Hook at An event x Outside; MCP server at Always-on x Outside.

elements.content: **Who triggers it?** Claude (from intent), you (explicitly), an event, or an always-on connection. **Where does it run?** The main chat, its own context, or outside the model.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- Walk the two axes once, slowly: trigger down the side, location across the top.

- Point out the placements: a skill and an agent are both Claude-triggered, but one stays in the main chat and one gets its own context. That single distinction is the next slide.

- A plugin is not on the grid: it is the box that ships these together.

elements.content: ## Which Tool? Two Columns Decide.

elements.alt: A decision table. Left column YOU WANT, right column REACH FOR A. Rows: follow your procedure when the topic comes up -> Skill; a scoped job done on its own returning a result -> Agent; a deliberate slash entry-point -> Command; something automatic on an event -> Hook; reach an external system -> MCP server; bundle and share any of the above -> Plugin. A dark rail reads Skill = knowledge, Agent = a worker, Command = a button, Hook = a tripwire, MCP = a wire outside, Plugin = the box.

elements.content: **Screenshot this one** -- it answers 'which tool?' once and for good.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- This is the durable takeaway of the session. Read each row as a sentence: 'I want X, so I reach for a Y.'

- Tell them to screenshot it; researchers stay confused about skill vs agent vs MCP long after a course ends, and this table is the cure.

- The bottom rail is the one-line mnemonic. Say it out loud.

elements.content: ## Skill vs Agent -- the Confusion Worth Clearing

elements.alt: Two panels. Left: MAIN CONVERSATION, a green scientific-figure skill card inside a chat bubble, labeled knowledge in context, Claude keeps working. Right: ITS OWN CONTEXT WINDOW, a purple figure-qa agent box with its own tools (Bash, Read, own model), labeled a worker, isolated, returns a result. A purple delegate arrow goes right and a green result arrow comes back.

elements.content: Both are triggered by Claude. **A skill is knowledge in the room you're already in; an agent is a separate worker with its own context and tools.** Reach for an agent when the work is isolated, repeatable, or runs in parallel.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- This is the number-one confusion. Use the Week 8 example: scientific-figure (skill) is procedure you use directly; figure-qa (agent) is a worker you hand a figure to and it reports back.

- The agent has its own context window -- that is why several can run at once and why you give it a restricted tool set.

- Rule of thumb on the callout: isolated, repeatable, or parallel -> agent; otherwise a skill.

elements.content: ## Skill vs MCP -- Knowledge vs a Wire

elements.alt: Claude on the left wired to an MCP server (red), which fans out to external systems on the right: MATLAB + EEGLAB (highlighted, labeled matlab-mcp, you drove this all course), a database, a web API, and a browser.

elements.content: A skill teaches Claude with tools it already has. **An MCP server gives it new tools by wiring to an external system.** You drove one the whole course: `matlab-mcp` for MATLAB + EEGLAB.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- The second confusion: skill vs MCP. Knowledge vs a connection.

- The course's own practicum is the proof: no skill could make Claude run MATLAB. It needed matlab-mcp, an MCP server -- a socket to the outside.

- Land the test: if the job needs the outside world (a program, a database, an API, a browser), it is an MCP server, not a skill.

elements.content: ## You Have Used All Six Already

elements.alt: A timeline of Weeks 1-9, each tagged by tool type: Wk1 git and Wk2 CLAUDE.md as foundation, Wk3 /epic-dev as Command, Wk4 pre-commit as Hook, Wk5 opencite, Wk6 grant, Wk7 manuscript as Skills, Wk8 figure-qa and Wk9 bids-validator as Agents. A red band spanning Wk3-9 marks matlab-mcp the MCP server; a blue band over Wk5-9 marks research-skills the plugin.

elements.content: Commands (Wk3), hooks (Wk4), skills (Wk5-9), agents (Wk8-9), an MCP server the whole practicum, all inside a plugin. **None of it was magic -- and today you author one.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- The graduation slide. Walk the timeline and name each week's tool type -- the colors match the legend.

- The point: they already trust every one of these. The building blocks are not new; only the names are.

- matlab-mcp ran underneath the practicum the whole time; research-skills shipped weeks 5-9. Today they build their own.

elements.content: ## The Plugin Is the Box; the Marketplace Is the Shelf

elements.alt: A blue plugin box titled plugin.json with compartments skills (green), agents (purple), commands (teal), hooks (amber), and an optional dashed MCP server config (red). An arrow points to a shelf titled marketplace.json holding plugin boxes labeled neuroinformatics, figures, manuscript, grant, with a note: 7 plugins in research-skills.

elements.content: One `plugin.json` bundles skills, agents, commands, and hooks. A marketplace lists them for install. **`research-skills` is one marketplace holding seven plugins.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- The plugin is the only one of the six that is a container, not a capability.

- plugin.json is the manifest; the folders are the compartments. An MCP server config can ride along too.

- A marketplace.json is just a list of plugins. research-skills is one marketplace with seven plugins -- the thing they have been installing since Week 5.

- Bridge: now let us open the simplest box -- a skill.

elements.content: ## What a Skill Actually Is

elements.alt: A directory tree for shot-change-erp with SKILL.md (required, frontmatter plus body) highlighted green, and optional references, scripts, and assets folders. Annotation cards explain: SKILL.md is the entry point always read first; references are detailed docs loaded only when needed; scripts are code executed token-free; assets are templates and icons used in output.

elements.content: An onboarding guide for a domain: a required `SKILL.md`, plus optional `references/`, `scripts/`, `assets/`. **Knowledge, packaged so it loads only when it is relevant.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- A skill turns general Claude into a specialist by handing over procedural knowledge no model fully has.

- Only SKILL.md is required. The three folders are optional and each has a job: references to read, scripts to run, assets to use in output.

- The mental frame to repeat all session: a skill is an onboarding guide written for the next Claude.

elements.content: ## Progressive Disclosure -- Why Skills Stay Cheap

elements.alt: A three-tier funnel. Tier 1: metadata, name plus description, always in context, about 100 words. Tier 2: SKILL.md body, loaded only when the skill triggers, target 1,500-2,000 words. Tier 3: references, scripts, assets, pulled only when needed, effectively unlimited, with a note that scripts run without being read into context.

elements.content: Metadata is always loaded; the body loads on trigger; resources load only when needed. **Keep SKILL.md lean; let references/ carry the weight.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- This is why you can install dozens of skills without drowning the context window.

- Three levels, loaded in order. Only the ~100-word metadata is always present; the body appears on trigger; the heavy material loads on demand.

- Practical rule: a fat SKILL.md taxes every trigger. Move detail to references/.

elements.content: ## The Trigger Description Decides Everything

elements.alt: A dissected description field showing third-person phrasing plus exact user phrases. Below, a green PASS card with a specific description full of concrete phrases that will fire, and a red FAIL card with vague descriptions like Provides guidance for figures that never load.

elements.content: Third person, full of the exact phrases users say. **A perfect skill with a vague description never fires.** The body is for when it loads; the description is whether it loads.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- The single highest-leverage line in the whole skill. If Claude cannot match a user message to your description, the skill is invisible.

- Two rules: third person ('This skill should be used when...') and the literal phrases people type.

- Show the PASS/FAIL contrast: concrete phrases fire; 'Provides guidance for figures' does nothing.

elements.content: ## Write for the Next Claude, in Imperative Form

elements.alt: Two columns. Left, crossed out: Writing for a human -- second person, prose, restates what a model knows, with a verbose example. Right, checked: Writing for the next Claude -- imperative, lean, only the non-obvious steps, with a terse example of epoching shot-onset events.

elements.content: A skill is onboarding for a future agent, not docs for a human. Imperative voice, no duplication, only the non-obvious. **If a capable model already knows it, leave it out.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- The non-obvious mindset shift: you are not documenting for a person, you are onboarding a future model.

- Imperative voice ('Read the file', not 'You should read the file'); no duplication between SKILL.md and references; skip what any capable model already knows.

- The right-hand example is what a good skill body looks like: terse, procedural, specific.

elements.content: ## /skill-development -- the Skill That Builds Skills

elements.alt: A six-step flow: 1 Understand (concrete example prompts), 2 Plan (scripts, references, assets), 3 Scaffold (mkdir skills/shot-change-erp), 4 Write (description plus lean SKILL.md body), 5 Validate (skill-reviewer agent), 6 Iterate (use, notice, refine), with a dashed loop arrow from step 6 back to step 4.

elements.content: Understand -> plan -> scaffold -> write -> validate -> iterate. **Steps 4-6 loop until it triggers cleanly.** It ships in the official `plugin-dev` plugin.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- You do not author skills from a blank file -- you run /skill-development, which is itself a skill.

- Six steps. The front three are planning; the back three are the real loop you live in.

- It is part of the official plugin-dev plugin, alongside agent-development, command-development, hook-development, mcp-integration, and plugin-structure.

elements.content: ## Test It: Does It Trigger?

elements.alt: Two test scenarios: an on-topic prompt 'analyze the shot-change ERPs' loads the shot-change-erp skill with a green check; an off-topic prompt 'what is the capital of France?' correctly loads no skill, also a check. Below, a terminal shows claude --plugin-dir ./my-plugin for local testing.

elements.content: The first test is loading, not prose. Say the phrase and watch it fire; test locally with `claude --plugin-dir`. **An untriggered skill is a skill that does not exist.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- The most common beginner mistake is polishing the body of a skill that never loads.

- Two-sided test: the right prompt fires it, and the wrong prompt does NOT. Both are passing.

- Test locally before publishing with claude --plugin-dir pointing at your plugin folder, then type the phrases and watch what loads.

elements.content: ## Review, Then Iterate

elements.alt: A purple skill-reviewer agent card emitting a checklist: description specific, body lean, detail in references, imperative writing, examples complete. To the right, a loop: use it for real, notice the struggle, revise by sharpening, moving, or adding a script, then use again.

elements.content: The `skill-reviewer` agent checks description, leanness, and disclosure. Then real use shows what to fix. **This is Week 10's mechanical defence** -- like cite-the-card (Wk5) and validate_fonts.py (Wk8).

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- Naturally, the thing that reviews a skill is an agent: skill-reviewer.

- It checks the mechanical stuff: is the description specific, is the body lean, is detail in references, is the writing imperative.

- Then the real loop is use-notice-revise. Every week had a mechanical defence; this is Week 10's.

elements.content: ## When a Skill Is Not Enough -- Level Up

elements.alt: A green Skill at the center with arrows to four targets: Agent (purple, a separate isolated worker, agent-development), Command (teal, a deliberate entry point, command-development), Hook (amber, automatic on every event, hook-development), and MCP server (red, a wire to an external system, mcp-integration). A note: all six live in plugin-dev.

elements.content: Need an isolated worker, an entry point, an event trigger, or an external connection? **Agent, command, hook, MCP** -- each has its own `plugin-dev` skill to guide you.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- A skill cannot do everything, and that is fine -- it is the starting point, not the ceiling.

- Map the four upgrades back to the decision table: isolated worker -> agent; deliberate entry point -> command; automatic on an event -> hook; external system -> MCP.

- Each has a plugin-dev skill that teaches it, the same way skill-development taught this one.

elements.content: ## Fork research-skills -- Make It Your Own

elements.alt: The upstream repo neuromechanist/research-skills (7 plugins, skills-first, BSD-3) with a Fork arrow to you/research-skills, whose plugins tree shows neuroinformatics/skills/bids-conversion and a highlighted new shot-change-erp skill folder.

elements.content: The marketplace you installed in Week 5 is a public repo. Fork it and it's yours: `plugins/*`, `marketplace.json`, `AGENTS.md`. **The tools you used all course are a repo you can own.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- The make-it-your-own move. research-skills is not a black box; it is a public GitHub repo.

- Fork it and you get the whole thing: seven plugins, the marketplace manifest, the cross-agent AGENTS.md.

- Note: we are NOT doing this live (demo risk); the commands are on the next two slides and in the blog.

elements.content: ## Add Your Skill, Install It Back

elements.alt: A four-step rail: 1 add the folder skills/shot-change-erp; 2 bump the version in plugin.json from 0.2.3 to 0.3.0; 3 claude plugin marketplace add ./research-skills; 4 claude plugin install neuroinformatics@research-skills. A green badge: AGENTS.md means the same skill works in Codex and Copilot CLI too.

elements.content: Drop the folder in, bump the version, `claude plugin marketplace add ./fork`, install. **Because of `AGENTS.md`, the same skill works in Codex and Copilot CLI too.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- Four steps from a folder on disk to an installed tool.

- The version bump matters: adding a skill is a minor bump (next slide).

- The cross-agent payoff is free: because instructions live in AGENTS.md, the same skill loads in Codex and Copilot CLI, not just Claude Code.

elements.content: ## Version It, Then Share It

elements.alt: Left: VERSION per plugin, the semver 0.3.0 with MAJOR breaking, MINOR new skill or plugin, PATCH edit within, plus rules: add a new skill is a minor bump, edit within a plugin is a patch bump. Right: SHARE three paths -- push your fork, open a pull request upstream, post it in the OSC Discord.

elements.content: Semver per plugin: a new skill is a minor bump, an edit is a patch. Then push your fork, open a pull request, or post in Discord. **A skill your lab can install beats a script in your downloads folder.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- Versioning is per plugin, not per marketplace. The two rules from the repo: new skill -> minor bump; edit within a plugin -> patch bump.

- Three ways to share, in increasing reach: your fork for your lab, a pull request to contribute upstream, or the OSC Discord for the community.

- The moral: a packaged, installable skill outlives a one-off script.

elements.content: ## Before You Build -- and When Not To

elements.alt: A pre-flight checklist: is there already a skill for this; is it knowledge, a connection, or an event; will I reuse it; is SKILL.md lean with detail in references; did I test that it triggers; any secrets or data (never). A red band: skip it if it is a one-off, it already exists, or it needs a secret.

elements.content: Already a skill? Knowledge, a connection, or an event? Will you reuse it? Any secrets (never)? **The bar is reuse: if you'll do it again, make it a skill.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- The honest counterweight to a whole session about building: do not over-build.

- Walk the checklist; the most important questions are 'does this already exist' and 'will I actually reuse it'.

- Hard rule: never put secrets or data in a skill -- it is shared knowledge, not a vault.

- The bar is reuse. A genuine one-off does not need to be a skill.

elements.content: ## Live Demo -- One Skill, Start to Finish

elements.alt: Two demo steps: 1 build with /skill-development, writing a strong trigger description and a lean SKILL.md for shot-change-erp (find shot events, epoch, average, plot), badged about 3 minutes; 2 prove it triggers by typing the phrase and watching the skill load, optionally running skill-reviewer, badged about 1 minute. A note: no live git fork, no install on stage.

elements.content: Build `shot-change-erp` with `/skill-development` (~3 min), then prove it triggers (~1 min). **No live fork or install -- a network hiccup won't derail the finale.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right to reveal the callout]

- Keep the demo small and honest. Two steps, about four minutes.

- Build a tiny shot-change-erp skill -- the analysis the course has run since Week 3 -- then prove it triggers by typing the natural-language phrase.

- The point is not a speed-run of a whole plugin; it is that building a skill is approachable. If validation surfaces something, we walk it; we do not manufacture a pass.

elements.content: ## Graduation -- What You Can Do Now

items.text: **From `git init` (Week 1) to a published skill (Week 10).** You used all six building blocks; today you built one.
**The decision table is yours:** skill / agent / command / hook / MCP / plugin, and which to reach for.
**The loop closes:** the HBN shot-change analysis you've grown since Week 3 is now a reusable skill anyone can install.
**You arrived a tool user. You leave a tool builder.**

elements.content:
**What's next:** build a skill for the chore you redo most; fork `research-skills`; contribute back; help the next cohort.

**Thank you for ten weeks. Questions while we build?**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Land the course in one breath: git init in Week 1 to a published skill in Week 10.

- The takeaways, one at a time: you used all six and built one; the decision table is yours; the practicum loop closes.

- The closing line is the thesis -- say it slowly, then pause: tool user in, tool builder out.

- Thank the cohort for ten weeks, then open the floor and start the live build.
